--- SOURCE 4: Scaling Field Data to Calibrate and Validate Moderate ... ---
URL: https://www.asprs.org/wp-content/uploads/pers/2007journal/august/2007_aug_945-954.pdf

SUMMARY:
by A Baccini · 2007 · Cited by 61 — Field data must be upscaled to match remote sensing resolution. This is done by spatially aggregating ground measurements using methods like landscape

FULL CONTENT:
Abstract Validation and calibration are essential components of nearly all remote sensing-based studies. In both cases, ground measurements are collected and then related to the remote sensing observations or model results. In many situations, and particularly in studies that use moderate resolution remote sensing, a mismatch exists between the sensor’s field of view and the scale at which in situ meas-urements are collected. The use of in situ measurements for model calibration and validation, therefore, requires a robust and defensible method to spatially aggregate ground measurements to the scale at which the remotely sensed data are acquired. This paper examines this challenge and specifically considers two different approaches for aggregat-ing field measurements to match the spatial resolution of moderate spatial resolution remote sensing data: (a) land-scape stratification; and (b) averaging of fine spatial resolu-tion maps. The results show that an empirically estimated stratification based on a regression tree method provides a statistically defensible and operational basis for performing this type of procedure.
Introduction Remote sensing is used routinely to map vegetation proper-ties over large areas. To calibrate remote sensing-based models and validate model results, field data are required (Ardo, 1992; Gemmell, 1995; Wulder, 1998; Franklin, 1986; Cohen and Spies, 1992; Danson and Curran, 1993; Puhr and Donoghue, 2000; Cohen et al., 2001; Cohen et al., 2003). For practical reasons, however, the number of data and the area sampled on the ground through fieldwork tends to be quite small. Indeed, the need to limit the cost of fieldwork over large areas often results in relatively small areas being sampled using field plots that are smaller than 1 ha. Thus, a central challenge in using field data in remote sensing-based studies is ensuring that the in situ measurements provide an appropriate and representative sample in support of the research or mapping goals.
The above problem is particularly acute in studies that encompass large areas using moderate spatial resolution remotely sensed data. Because moderate spatial resolution Scaling Field Data to Calibrate and Validate Moderate Spatial Resolution Remote Sensing Models A. Baccini, M.A. Friedl, C.E. Woodcock, and Z. Zhu sensors provide data with spatial resolutions on the order of 100’s of meters, the ground area covered by a pixel typically consists of a mosaic of land-cover types. Thus, the radiance measured by the sensor reflects the combined properties of this mosaic. In this situation, it is important to consider how the area sampled on the ground is related to the instanta-neous-field-of-view measured by remote sensing. Specifically, to use plot-scale data to calibrate or validate moderate spatial resolution remote sensing models, it is necessary to upscale the field data to match the spatial resolution of the remote sensing observations.
Despite its importance, the above issue has received relatively little attention. Recently, however, a variety of papers have identified it as a key problem, particularly in regards to validation activities. For example, to minimize the effect of mixed pixels in validation efforts for products generated from the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectrora-diometer (MODIS), Milne and Cohen (1999) suggested locat-ing field plots in areas with low spatial variance in Landsat data. In a related study conducted in Botswana, Tian et al.
(2002) addressed the problem of validating estimates of leaf area index (LAI) derived from MODIS using field plots ranging from 34 to 2,756 m2. Because the scale at which the field data were collected was not consistent with the spatial resolution of MODIS data, a direct comparison was not feasible. As a solution, Tian et al. (2002) used Landsat data to identify homogeneous areas on the basis of spectral similarity and adjacency using a segmentation algorithm.
These regions were then used to aggregate the field data to the scale of the MODIS pixels.
The research reported in this paper extends these efforts and specifically considers the following question: how can data collected at the scale of field plots be aggregated and related to remote sensing measurements collected at much coarser spatial resolutions such that fine-scale spatial variation in surface properties does not corrupt or reduce the validity of the analysis? To answer this question, we examine several methods to spatially aggregate biophysical measurements collected in field surveys to the scale of moderate spatial resolution remotely sensed data. Here, we examine the specific problem of mapping biomass. However, the general issues and PHOTOGRAMMETRIC ENGINEERING & REMOTE SENSING August 2007 945 A. Baccini, M.A. Friedl, and C.E. Woodcock are with the Department of Geography and Center for Remote Sensing, Boston University, 675 Commonwealth Avenue, Boston, MA 02215 (abaccini@bu.edu).
Z. Zhu is with the USGS-EROS Data Center, Sioux Falls, SD.
Photogrammetric Engineering & Remote Sensing Vol. 73, No. 8, August 2007, pp. 945–954.
0099-1112/07/7308–0945/$3.00/0 © 2007 American Society for Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing methods that we consider are applicable to many other applications in which field data are used to calibrate remote sensing models or validate maps of surface biophys-ical variables over large areas.
Data Study Area The study area used for this research encompasses the northeastern region of Utah and is delineated by one Landsat ETM scene (path 37, row 32). The area is characterized by conifer forest (Douglas fir, ponderosa pine, subalpine fir, Engelmann spruce, blue spruce, white fir, and lodgepole pine), hardwood forest (aspen and birch), and shrub forma-tions with juniper, limber pine, bristlecone pine, gamble oak, and Rocky Mountain Maple.
The elevation varies from about 1,300 m to 4,000 m above sea level, and the total annual average precipitation varies from about 20 cm to 115 cm. Climate data were provided by the gridded data set developed by Thornton et al. (1997) and are representative of the 18-year period from 1980 to 1997. This data set provides mean monthly temperature and precipitation data at a spatial resolution of 1 km2. Elevation data are from the USGS 1-arc-second National Elevation Dataset.
Field Data The United States Forest Service (USFS) in Utah provided the field data used for this analysis. Specifically, we used data collected through the USFS Forest Inventory and Analysis (FIA) Program, which collects and reports forest inventory information for forested land in the United States. The FIA program employs statistical sampling using field plots, where biophysical variables (e.g., tree height and diameter at breast height (DBH)) are measured directly in the field. FIA data, therefore, provide a reliable source of forest inventory data at the local, regional, and national scale.
The FIA sampling design is based on a two-phase system-atic grid. In phase one, remote sensing interpretation defines the extent and distribution of forest cover. In the second phase, field plots are selected (roughly one every 2,428 hectares) and detailed forest structure and composition measurements are made. Each plot consists of a cluster of four circular subplots with fixed radii, where each of the four subplots includes approximately 167 m2 (radius  7.3 m). Three subplots are located 36.6 m away from the center of a central sub-plot, at azimuths of 120, 240, and 360 degrees, covering a total area of about 0.6 hectares. For more information see Frayer and Furnival (1999).
A “land condition information” variable provided for each FIA plot indicates if the plot falls in forested land, non-forested land, or water. For this work, only forest plots were considered, which provided a total sample of 437 plots.
Of primary importance were the total basal area, which is defined as the total basal area of live trees with DBH greater than or equal to 2.5 cm, and the weighted average tree height for live trees with DBH greater than or equal to 2.5 cm, where the weights are proportional to the tree basal area. Measurements of above-ground biomass were not available and were therefore calculated as the product of total basal area, the weighted average tree height, and a biomass conversion factor. This approach is used widely (Brown et al., 1989; Schroeder et al., 1997) and provides an accurate approximation to timber volume which is normally calculated with a similar allometric equation that accounts for tree shape.
Remote Sensing Data We used two main sources of remote sensing data. First, Landsat Enhanced Thematic Mapper (ETM) data were used to characterize landscape-scale spatial variation in forest composition and structure. Second, moderate spatial resolu-tion (1 km2) data were provided by MODIS.
A set of three Landsat ETM images acquired on 06 July 1999, 30 January 2000, and 08 May 2001 were used. Prepro-cessing of these images was performed as part of the Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics Consortium (MRLC) at the USGS EROS Data Center (Irish, 2000). To exploit information related to seasonal vegetation dynamics separate images from spring, summer, and winter were used. These images were radiometrically and geometrically corrected according to methods developed at the USGS EROS Data Center (Irish, 2000). Geometric rectification was performed (including terrain correction) using the USGS 1-arc-second National Elevation Dataset.
For each Landsat ETM scene, the MRLC provides images based on the Tasselled Cap transformation (bright-ness, greenness, and wetness), in addition to the raw spectral data. To reduce the dimensionality of the data set principal components were computed using spectral data from all three dates using all of the Landsat ETM bands, excluding the thermal band. The first six components, which explained 96 percent of the variance in the data, were used in the analysis presented below.
The MODIS data used for this work consisted of 1 km nadir bidirectional reflectance distribution function- (BRDF) adjusted surface reflectance (NBAR). Each NBAR image provides surface reflectance data that have been normalized to a consistent nadir view geometry and are atmospheri-cally corrected, cloud-cleared, and representative of 16-day periods. Complete details regarding the MODIS NBAR product are provided in Schaaf et al. (2002).
Analysis The conceptual approach that was used in this study is presented in Figure 1. The basic challenge that we address is how to most accurately scale data collected in the field to match remotely sensed measurements made at much coarser spatial resolutions: in this case, 1 km MODIS data. As we 946 August 2007 PHOTOGRAMMETRIC ENGINEERING & REMOTE SENSING Figure 1. Flowchart showing the different methods presented in this paper to scale field data.
describe in Figure 1, this is essentially a problem of optimal spatial aggregation. For this work, we examined three different methods to spatially aggregate (or scale) plot-scale field data to spatial scales that are commensurate with moderate spatial resolution remotely sensed data. These methods fall into two main classes: (a) averaging of fine spatial resolution maps produced from Landsat ETM data, and (b) area-weighted averaging of plot data based on landscape stratification.
Spatial Averaging of High Spatial Resolution Maps Ordinary least squares (OLS) methods have been used widely to relate remotely sensed data to field-measured biophysical variables (e.g., Cohen et al., 2003). To do this, spectral indices such as the normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) are commonly employed as regressor variables (e.g., Friedl et al., 1994).
More complex indices derived from transformations of spectral data such as brightness, greenness, and wetness indices are also frequently employed, and principal compo-nent analysis has also been used to decrease the dimension-ality of multi-temporal data sets for use in regression modeling (Cohen et al., 2001).
Despite their widespread use the utility of OLS methods is limited by several factors. First, they assume that the relationship between the response variable and the regressor variable(s) is linear. Second, they assume that the regressor variable(s) is (are) measured perfectly (i.e., with no error).
Third, if more than one regressor variable is used (e.g., multispectral imagery), then model selection can become quite challenging. Together, these issues seriously constrain the utility of OLS methods and care must therefore be taken when applying them. More complete discussions regarding the issues associated with using OLS methods in remote sensing are presented in Curran and Hay (1986) and Fernandes and Leblanc (2005).
Here, we assess the utility of OLS methods using two different OLS approaches. First, we use univariate regression to predict biomass as a function of NDVI derived from ETM data. Second, we use multiple linear regression where the regressor variables were selected using a stepwise (backward and forward) approach based on the AIC criterion. The “best” model was then selected based on the resulting R2, adjusted-R2, and prediction error sum of squares (PRESS) index including consideration of the so-called variance inflation factor (VIF), which was used to evaluate multicollinearity among the regressors (Montgomery et al., 2001).
Landscape Stratification and Spatially-Weighted Averaging The landscape stratification approach used in this paper follows the strategy described in Davis et al. (1992), Milne and Cohen (1999), and Tian et al. (2002). This approach assumes that the land surface can be stratified into units that are relatively uniform with regards to the variable of interest.
Unlike the work of Milne and Cohen (1999) and Tian et al.
(2002), this research assumes that the landscape can be stratified in a spatially exhaustive fashion. Therefore, statistical methods associated with stratified random sam-pling can be used to assess the effectiveness of this approach (Cochran, 1977). Below we describe and test two main approaches to landscape stratification: (a) using existing vegetation maps as a basis for stratification, and (b) using a regression tree-based stratification estimated using field data in conjunction with remote sensing, climate, and topographic data that is optimized to map spatial variance in biomass.
Vegetation Maps Existing vegetation maps provide a simple and intuitive basis for spatially averaging biophysical measurements collected in field inventories. For this work, we used an existing vegetation map for the study site provided by the LANDFIRE project (EROS, 2003). To aggregate the FIA plot data to the scale of MODIS pixels, the vegetation map was intersected with the FIA data and mean biomass values were computed for each vegetation class. Using these values as stratum means, spatial averaging was accomplished by computing an area-weighted average of the stratum means for each stratum present in each MODIS pixel. This approach was then assessed by quantifying the proportion and statistical significance of spatial variance in biomass explained by the stratification.
Regression Tree Stratification The relationship between climate and terrestrial vegetation is well-established and has been exploited by biogeogra-phers to map vegetation for decades (Holdridge, 1947; Box, 1981; Walter, 1979; Woodward, 1987; Prentice et al., 1992).
Similarly, climate and topographic variables have been used widely in association with remotely sensed data to increase the accuracy of maps of vegetation composition and struc-ture (Strahler, 1980). For example, topographic data (eleva-tion, slope, and transformed aspect) have been used as a surrogate for microclimate and combined with Landsat data to map forest vegetation in California (Franklin et al., 1986; Woodcock et al., 1994; Franklin et al., 2000). Having said this, the relationship between forest properties and climate, elevation, and spectral information is complex and often non-linear (Baccini et al., 2004). As a result, parametric statistical methods often fail to characterize the magnitude and form of relationships among these variables.
For this work, we used the approach described by Michaelsen et al. (1994), where regression tree analysis is used to produce a landscape stratification using remote sensing, climate, and terrain variables. Specifically, we used biomass as the response variable and ETM, climate and topographic data as predictors. Because all of the predictor variables were available in a spatially continuous fashion (i.e., everywhere in the study region), the resulting regression tree was used as a basis for landscape stratification following the method described by Michaelsen et al. (1994). The results from this analysis were then used to aggregate the FIA plot data to the scale of 1 km2 MODIS pixels following the area-weighted averaging procedure previously described.
Results Regression Model Results To assess the OLS model approach, we split the available FIA data into two sets, where roughly 23 percent of the data (101 sites) were selected at random and used as independent test data. Results from the univariate regression analysis using NDVI derived from ETM data (spring date) show that NDVI explained only 24 percent of the variance in biomass with a root-mean-squared-error (RMSE)  81 tonnes ha1 for the independent data set. A multiple linear regression explained 47 percent of the variance (RMSE  71.9 tonnes ha1) when applied to the independent data set. However, four out of the nine predictor variables possessed a VIF larger than 10, which indicates a high degree of multicollinearity in the model. It is therefore likely that the estimated R2 is inflated. Figure 2 presents scatterplots of observed versus predicted biomass for both regression models for the independent data set and clearly illustrates the level of uncertainty in these models.
Perhaps more importantly, inspection of diagnostic plots (Figure 3) for the estimated models reveals non-constant variance in the model residuals, a serious violation of model assumptions that makes prediction on unseen cases using PHOTOGRAMMETRIC ENGINEERING & REMOTE SENSING August 2007 947 948 August 2007 PHOTOGRAMMETRIC ENGINEERING & REMOTE SENSING Figure 2. (a) Predicted versus observed values based on univariate OLS with NDVI. (b) Observed versus predicted values based on multivariate OLS.
Figure 3. (a) Diagnostic plots for univariate OLS with NDVI. (b) Diagnostic plots for multivariate OLS.
PHOTOGRAMMETRIC ENGINEERING & REMOTE SENSING August 2007 949 TABLE 1.

ALL F VALUES ARE STATISTICALLY SIGNIFICANT (PF  0.001). this model problematic. We, therefore, do not consider OLS methods further.
Regression Tree Analysis The regression tree analysis was performed using ETM, elevation, and climate data to predict biomass. To determine the optimal tree size, two different analyses were performed.
First, a 10-fold cross-validation procedure was used in which large regression trees were grown and successively pruned back.
The optimal tree was selected based on the tree size that explained the most variance in unseen data. Second, we visually inspected stratifications produced using trees of different sizes. In both cases, the results were tested using a leave-one-out cross-validation approach.
Table 1 presents results from this analysis for tree sizes of 2, 4, 8, 12, and 17 terminal nodes. For comparison, we include results from a one-way analysis of variance where the data were stratified using the vegetation map. The resulting regression trees explained 29 to 40 percent of the variance in the unseen data. According to these results, the optimal tree size was eight strata. It is interesting to notice that the regression trees approach even with a smaller number of strata compared to the vegetation stratification (for example: 8 strata versus 10 strata), explains more variance than the vegetation map approach. A visual inspection of the strata map derived from the regression tree stratification suggested that a stratification with 17 strata captured important additional spatial variability, with relatively little loss in explanatory power.
Comparison of Stratification Methods To evaluate the effectiveness of the regression tree approach versus using the existing LANDFIRE vegetation map as a basis for stratification, we computed the area-weighted mean, the RMSE, and the area-weighted variance of the mean following the approach used by Davis et al. (1992). Results from this comparison are presented in Table 2. For the regression tree analysis we present results for two sets of data: one that was used for estimating the regression tree, and a second data set that was held out and used as an independent basis for evaluating the effectiveness of the regression tree approach. This was not required for the stratification based on the LANDFIRE map. To allow comparison of results across methods we, therefore, present these results for three distinct data sets: (a) the entire data set, (b) the training set, and (c) the test set.
The results presented in Table 2 show that the esti-mated variance in the mean is 14 tonnes ha1 for the regression tree stratification, and about 30 tonnes ha1 for the stratification based on the vegetation map. These results, in combination with those presented in Table 2 suggest that the regression tree approach provides a significantly more accurate stratification with respect to biomass. Note that two strata are missing in the test set for the regression tree stratification, which likely explains the difference in the area-weighted average between the training and testing sets.
In particular, the high biomass stratum is not present in the test set, which biases the estimated average. Note also that the area-weighted means are systematically lower when estimated using the regression tree stratification. The key result, however, is that the uncertainty in the mean based on the regression tree stratification is reduced relative to that estimated using the vegetation map.
Table 3 reports the mean and standard deviation in biomass along with the vegetation classes included and the number of observations (field plots) for each biomass stratum identified by the regression tree. Table 4 reports the percent area occupied, the average biomass, and the number of plots within each vegetation class. The fact that more than one vegetation class is embedded in all except one stratum identified by the regression tree demonstrates that different vegetation classes have approximately the same biomass (and vice versa), which reduces the utility of the vegetation map as a basis for stratifying landscapes with regards to biomass. In contrast, the regression tree-based stratification is specifically optimized to explain variance in biomass and, therefore, should provide a TABLE 2.

Mean Training set 336 RT 17 60.5 140 44 13.5 Test set 101 RT 15 81.8 126 55 14.6 Training set 336 VM 10 97.9 163 61 32.4 Test set 101 VM 9 129.5 131 66 24.4 Train  Test 437 RT 17 61.0 136 50 16.2 Train  Test 437 VM 10 95.38 157 61 30.45 aRT indicates stratification based on the regression tree approach; VM indicates stratification derived from the vegetation map.
superior basis for spatial aggregation of plot measurements of biomass.
Figure 4 presents boxplots showing the distribution of biomass among the different strata derived from the regres-sion tree and vegetation map stratifications. This figure shows that the regression-tree-based stratification captures the range of spatial variability in biomass within the study area much more effectively than the vegetation map: the aggregation based on the vegetation map has a much narrower biomass range, while the RT-based stratification retains the range of the original distribution much more closely. Note, however, that there is significant overlap among strata. This occurs because two or more strata may have roughly the same mean and range in biomass, but belong to two different landscapes. For example, low to intermediate biomass may reflect mature stands with widely spaced trees or (alternatively) less mature stands consisting of densely spaced younger trees. Therefore, while the mean and variance of individual strata can be quite similar, they can be quite different in terms of their biophysical and ecological properties.
950 August 2007 PHOTOGRAMMETRIC ENGINEERING & REMOTE SENSING Figure 4. Boxplots of biomass (tonnes ha1) stratified using regression trees with 10 and 17 terminal nodes, and using the vegetation map: (a) Regression Tree - 10 Strata, (b) Regression Tree - 17 Strata, and (c) Vegetation Map - 10 Strata. Boxes show the median (white horizontal line), quartiles (solid horizontal line), range (verti-cal dotted line), outliers (horizontal lines), and notches, which indicate approximate 95 percent confidence intervals for the mean of each stratum. The width of each box is proportional to the square root of the number of observations in the box. The x axis reports the average biomass (tonnes ha1) of the strata.
Spatial Aggregation The results presented above provide a comparison of different strategies for stratifying landscapes with respect to biomass. The objective of this work, however, was to investigate methods for spatially aggregating field data to the scale of remotely sensed observations. Thus, the ultimate test regarding the effectiveness of any such method is the degree to which the spatially aggregated field data provide an accurate representation of the spatially averaged variable.
In the absence of much more extensive field data, this representation is impossible to test. However, an indirect way to assess this is to examine the degree to which the spatially averaged data retain the frequency distribution of the original field data. Of course, spatial aggregation will act to smooth the data, but if we can assume that (a) the field data are representative, (b) the stratification is effective at capturing spatial variance, and (c) the scale of spatial variance is not excessively fine, the basic statistical proper-ties of the aggregated data should not be significantly altered.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Field trials validate remote sensing data by collecting ground measurements, ensuring that observed satellite data accurately represents actual agricultural yields.
By comparing remote sensing results with historical yield data, researchers can assess the reliability and consistency of yield forecasts.
Accuracy assessments involve statistical analyses to quantify discrepancies between remote sensing predictions and real-world observations, enhancing the credibility of yield forecasts.
